title: SEGMENT 16 — Phage as research tools: still the workhorse

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Phage as research tools: still the workhorse

Let me bring us back, for a moment, to the laboratory use of phages — because while phage therapy and food safety get the headlines, phages remain a foundational research tool, and a sense of the breadth of that use is part of the field's portrait.

We covered phage display extensively in its own lecture, so I'll just recap: M13 phage is the dominant display platform, used in essentially every antibody discovery campaign, every peptide screening, every protein-protein interaction mapping.
The 2018 Nobel Prize.
Humira and a dozen other antibody drugs.
Beyond display, phages serve as cloning vectors.
Lambda was, for decades, the workhorse cloning vector for inserts too large for plasmids — you could clone forty kilobases of foreign DNA into a lambda vector and propagate it.
Cosmids and phagemids — hybrids combining phage and plasmid features — extended the range further.
The first generation of complete genome libraries — including the Human Genome Project's early libraries — used phage-derived vectors.
Modern cloning has largely moved on to BAC and YAC vectors for big inserts and standard plasmids for small inserts, but the conceptual lineage runs through phage.
Phages are delivery vehicles for genome engineering experiments.
The most common technique for moving DNA between E. coli strains, called transduction, uses phages — typically lambda or P1 — to package and transfer bacterial DNA.
P1 transduction has been a standard tool of bacterial genetics for sixty years and is still used routinely.

The technique simply exploits the fact that phages occasionally make packaging errors and put bacterial DNA into a phage capsid instead of phage DNA — and when that mispackaged particle infects a new cell, it injects the bacterial DNA, which can recombine into the recipient's chromosome.
Phages are selection systems.
The CRISPR screening field uses phage challenge as one method of selecting bacteria with desired genetic properties — you infect a library with phage and recover the survivors.

Phage-assisted continuous evolution — PACE, developed by David Liu's lab — uses phage-host coupling as a directed evolution tool, where the phage's reproduction depends on a function you want to evolve, so phages with that function out-compete others.
PACE has been used to evolve novel protein binders, T7 RNA polymerases with new promoter specificities, base editors with optimized properties.
It's a powerful framework, and it sits squarely on phage biology.
Phages are single-virus genetic dissection systems — meaning, because of the plaque assay, you can do genetics on individual viral particles in a way you can't easily do with most other organisms.
You can mutate, plate, pick single plaques, characterize them, recombine them in defined crosses.
The genetic toolkit developed for lambda — temperature-sensitive mutants, amber suppressors, mutant complementation — is unrivaled in any other virus.
For a graduate student learning rigorous genetics, doing a few experiments on lambda is still considered a useful rite of passage in some labs.
So phages are still, eighty years after Delbrück and Luria, the workhorses of a remarkable range of research.
The reason is simple: they're small, they're tractable, they're fast, they're cheap, and the genetic and molecular toolkits around them are extraordinarily mature.
New applications keep emerging.
The phage will likely remain a fundamental research tool for as long as we keep doing molecular biology.
